entry.title: SAP Identity Provisioning Service - All Available Connectors for User sync from Source to Target
entry.content:
Why this matters. In a top‑down user provisioning model, you drive identities from one (or a few) authoritative sources into many downstream business applications. SAP Identity Provisioning (IPS) acts as the orchestration layer: it reads users/groups/roles from sources, optionally transforms them, and writes them into targets.

Picking the right connector—and knowing what each one supports—lets you design flows that are robust, secure, and easy to maintain.
 

How to read the catalog

Each connector below lists:

  • Display Name & Type – How it appears in IPS and the internal connector key you reference in jobs.
  • Destination Type – The protocol or API family IPS uses (HTTP, LDAP, RFC, SSH, etc.).
  • Supported Entities – What IPS can read from that system in a top‑down context (e.g., user, group, role, or event).
  • Visibility / Enabled – Whether the connector is visible in the UI and currently enabled.
  • Destination Required – Whether the connector expects a dedicated “Destination” object (e.g., RFC for ABAP).

Tip: For enterprise-scale top‑down setups, a common pattern is Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) or Identity Authentication (IAS) as the authoritative source, fanning out to S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Fieldglass, SAP BTP services, and any non‑SAP apps via SCIM or LDAP. Use IPS transformations (mappings, filters) to normalize attributes across targets.

SAP IPS Connector - Supported Systems

#Connector TypeDisplay NameDestination TypeSupported EntitiesVisibleRequiredEnabled Destination

2ADVANCED_FINANCIAL_CLOSINGSAP Advanced Financial ClosingHTTPuser, groupTrueTrueFalse

entry.title: How to Lookup Employee Info in SuccessFactors Business Rule?
entry.content:
Background

The Lookup() function is one of the most frequently used features in both Excel and SAP SuccessFactors business rules.
In Employee Central (EC), common scenarios include:

  • Looking up entitled annual leave based on seniority maintained in a custom MDF object
  • Performing value replacement or mapping using a custom MDF configuration table
    In most standard use cases, Lookup works smoothly — as long as the target object is MDF-based.

Challenge

However, limitations appear when attempting cross-module or cross-entity lookups.
Typical challenges include:

  1. HRIS elements (e.g., EmpJob, User) are not supported as Lookup targets
    The Lookup function only supports MDF objects. Direct lookup on HRIS tables such as empJob or user is not allowed.
  2. Effective-dated logic constraints
    SuccessFactors Business rules only return the latest record as of today if using MDF or other as start navigation entity, making it difficult to retrieve employee information for a specific historical period.
    These restrictions become more visible in cross-module scenarios.

Business Requirements

Scenario 1 – Recruiting

In Recruiting, you may need to retrieve the contact information of a specific HR personnel who is not configured as a standard operator in the job requisition.
As we know:

  • The number of standard operators is limited
  • You cannot navigate to arbitrary employee entities in Recruiting rules execpt the pre-defined operators (e.g., Recruiter, Hiring Manager)
  • Auto-populating HR contact information becomes difficult

Scenario 2 – Identifying Manager Role

A customer needs to determine whether an employee is a manager.
However:

  • There is no standard boolean field in Employment Information that directly identifies “Manager”
  • The information may exist in Position or Job Information
  • It is not easily accessible via Lookup in business rules

Technical Solution

Considering the following facts:

  1. Lookup only supports MDF objects, not HRIS elements
  2. Integration Center is a built-in lightweight middleware tool
  3. SuccessFactors can act as both Source and Target system
    A practical workaround is:

Replicate key HRIS data (e.g., Job Information) into a custom MDF object using a scheduled Integration Center job.
This makes the data accessible through Lookup in business rules.

Step 1 – Create a Custom MDF Object

Design an MDF object to mirror required EC fields.

Key Design Principles

  • externalCode must be String type
  • Use an onSave rule to auto-populate:
    userId + "_" + effectiveStartDate
  • This ensures uniqueness for effective-dated records

Recommended Fields

You could add the custom fields based on business requirement.

Field IDType Source Mapping (empJob)
externalCodeStringuserId + effectiveDate
effectiveStartDateDatestartDate
cust_endDateDateendDate
cust_managerIdStringmanagerId

Step 2 – Create an Integration Center Job

Integration Design

  • Source System: SuccessFactors
  • Target System: SuccessFactors
  • A single scheduled job is sufficient.

Key Configuration Points

  • Starting Entity: empJob
    This covers global assignment and multiple employment scenarios via userId.
  • Advanced Filter: effectiveLatestChange eq true
    Ensures only the latest change on the same effective date is selected.
  • Time Filter:
    Set "From Date" = 1900-01-01
    This ensures historical records are included.
  • Schedule:
    Daily job (recommended during off-peak hours)

Result

This approach provides a lightweight, stable, and scalable workaround within standard SuccessFactors capabilities to effectively converts non-supported HRIS data into rule-friendly MDF data, after replication:

  • The custom MDF object becomes available in Business Rules
  • Lookup can retrieve cross-module employee information
  • Effective-dated records are preserved
  • Complex navigation limitations are avoided

entry.title: Re: Any standard utility in ABAP to retrieve a version of...
entry.content:
I guess you're interested only by the last version in PROD, and you want to copy it from PROD to DEV. For the last version and for any kind of object, possibly SVRS_GET_VERSION_REMOTE. NB: if the question had been about retrieving any version remotely, I don't know.

For copying code of the active version, I recommend using a transport of copies, but you must copy the whole object (R3TR). For instance, if you find out that only one method of a class is different, you'll have to transport the whole class. Your DEV system must be configured with VERS_AT_IMP = ALWAYS to keep the version history. In case your object source system is not DEV (the system ID of your DEV system), I recommend changing it to DEV.

The function modules for working with versions and transport requests are not released by SAP so you may use them at your own risk.

What you could do:

  1. I think it's best to create an RFC-enabled Z function module to be installed on your PROD system, which calls:
    1. TRINT_INSERT_COMM to create a ToC,
    2. TRINT_RELEASE_REQUEST to release the ToC. There's BAPI_CTREQUEST_RELEASE too but it has limited features.
    3. Read the created files in the cofiles and data directories (be careful the release may run asynchronously, so you may need to wait a little bit), return the data from these files.
  2. In the DEV system:
    1. Create these files in the cofiles and data directories of your DEV system (if the transport domain of DEV and PRD are different),
    2. Add the transport request to the import queue via TMS_MGR_GREP_TRANSPORT_QUEUE and TMS_MGR_FORWARD_TR_REQUEST
    3. Import via TMS_MGR_IMPORT_TR_REQUEST.
    4. NB: you may get the transport domain via TMS_CFG_GET_DOMAIN_NAME.

NB: I never saw a customer asking for such a risky program (automating things between PROD and DEV systems -- the PROD system should never be connected to a non-production system for security reason --, overwriting objects without control) and I would never suggest doing this, so it's really at your own risk.

Make sure that the client understands the risks.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)
entry.content:
PLEASE NOTE: Selecting the ‘Will you be attending?’ option will NOT register you for the event.

To register for this event, please click here: Register Here

SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)

This session will help you understand the relevant components of an adequate adoption program to maximize your cloud investment and ensure sustained business value. We will walk you through the essentials to orchestrate Change Management, Governance, Sustainment, and Value Management and how they work together to drive ongoing adoption, solution optimization and user experience.​ Participants will explore best practices and take away actionable templates to build an initial transformation roadmap, define clear roles and responsibilities, and identify the right executive sponsors and stakeholder groups.​ This webcast is a foundational session that will lead into optional follow‑on topic specific webcasts on your preferred topics.. At the end, you will leave with a concrete structure and toolkit to start or refine your own SuccessFactors adoption program.

Please contact SAP_SuccessFactors_Onboarding@sap.com for any questions on this event.

Here is a link to our Global schedule

entry.title: Re: how to give new line in segment E1BPSDTEXT of idoc SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2
entry.content: I couldn't find any SAP note so I advise contacting SAP Support. A workaround is to call the function modules INIT_TEXT, SAVE_TEXT and COMMIT_TEXT directly (only SAVE_TEXT is mandatory, the two others depend on the parameters you passed to SAVE_TEXT), followed by a COMMIT WORK.

entry.title: From Spreadsheets to a Financial Digital Twin: Reinventing FP&A with SAP
entry.content:
What Exactly Is a Financial Digital Twin?

At its core, a financial digital twin is a virtual model of your financial reality that is:

  • Connected: Continuously fed by actuals and operational data.
  • Intelligent: Built on business drivers and assumptions, not just static accounts.
  • Interactive: Able to simulate scenarios and show impacts on P&L, balance sheet, and cash in near real time.

You can think of it as a flight simulator for your finance function: instead of testing strategies in the real world (with real risk), you run them through the twin and see how they behave under different conditions.

Why CFOs and FP&A Leaders Should Care

  1. From Static Budgets to Continuous Planning

Traditional FP&A runs on an annual budget and a handful of reforecasts. A digital twin enables:

  • Rolling forecasts that update when new data arrives, not just at quarter-end.
  • Near real-time visibility into how operational changes (orders, production, collections) flow into revenue, margins, and cash.

The result is a finance function that reacts to shocks (demand drops, price spikes, supply disruptions) in days, not months.

2. Better Decisions Through Scenario-Based Thinking

A financial digital twin is built for “what if” questions:

  • What if volume drops by 10% in our top three markets?
  • What if we move 15% of sales to a lower-margin channel?
  • What if FX moves against us by 5% for two quarters?

Instead of debating assumptions in PowerPoint, you run scenarios in the twin and see quantified impacts on earnings, leverage, and liquidity. This supports:

  • More confident guidance to the board and investors.
  • Better capital allocation and risk-management decision

 

3. A Shared View Across Finance and Operations

In many organizations, operations and finance look at different numbers and speak different languages. The digital twin creates a shared environment where:

  • Operations sees how their KPIs (throughput, yield, service levels) hit EBITDA and cash.
  • Finance sees the operational levers behind variances.

This alignment turns FP&A into a collaborative business partner rather than a reporting factory.

External Data ( machine info, Forex data etc)

The SAP Building Blocks of a Financial Digital Twin

If your core runs on SAP, you already have most of the building blocks. The opportunity is to deliberately assemble them into a coherent digital twin architecture.

  1. SAP S/4HANA (and Central Finance): The Single Source of Truth

The Universal Journal in S/4HANA gives you a single, granular source of financial and controlling data. This is the transactional backbone of your twin:

  • All postings end up in one place (ACDOCA) with rich dimensions (company code, profit center, segment, customer, product, etc.).
  • You can drill from consolidated views down to individual line items without leaving the system.

If you run multiple ERPs, Central Finance can pull these into a harmonized S/4HANA-based finance hub. The digital twin then represents the full group, not just one system.

2. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): Planning, Forecasting, and Decision Cockpit

SAC is where much of the FP&A “front end” of the twin lives:

  • Planning models aligned to S/4HANA dimensions and hierarchies.
  • Driver-based budgeting where users adjust volumes, prices, and headcount instead of typing values into every account.
  • Scenario management with versions for base, upside, downside, and stress-test cases.
  • Dashboards and stories to visualize actuals, plan, and forecast side by side.

With live connectivity to S/4HANA, SAC can show up-to-date actuals while keeping planning data in the same space, eliminating reconciliation headaches.

3. SAP PaPM: The Economic Engine

A true digital twin needs to reflect how value is actually created and consumed, not just how it is reported. SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) helps by:

  • Modeling complex cost and revenue allocation logic (cost-to-serve, shared services, transfer pricing).
  • Simulating the impact of changes in drivers (volumes, discounts, service levels) on product and customer profitability.

PaPM can feed its results back into SAC planning and S/4HANA reporting, turning your twin into a realistic economic model, not just a spreadsheet on steroids.

4. Group Reporting: Real-Time Group Perspective

For group-level FP&A, S/4HANA Group Reporting is critical:

  • It consolidates group financials close to the source, enabling faster closes and near real-time group views.
  • Integrated with SAC, it allows group-level planning and scenario modeling on top of live consolidated data.

This closes the gap between statutory reporting, management reporting, and FP&A scenarios.

5. SAP Signavio: The Process Twin

Finance outcomes are driven by processes: order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, record-to-report, and forecast-to-fulfill. SAP Signavio adds a process-intelligence layer:

  • Process mining shows how these processes really run (cycle times, rework, bottlenecks).
  • You can link these insights to financial impacts—e.g., how process delays affect DSO, inventory, and working capital.

When you overlay Signavio insights onto your financial twin, FP&A can model not just financial numbers but the process changes needed to achieve them.

 

 

 

Putting It Together: An SAP-Based Financial Digital Twin Architecture

At a high level, your architecture might look like this:

  1. Data foundation
    • S/4HANA (and Central Finance if needed) holds harmonized actuals and master data.
    • Group Reporting provides consolidated views.
  2. Modeling and simulation
    • PaPM and CO-PA model profitability and key drivers.
    • Signavio models and analyses process flows.
  3. Planning and analytics
    • SAC hosts planning models, scenarios, dashboards, and predictive analytics.
  4. User experience
    • FP&A, business controllers, and business leaders access insights mainly through SAC and Fiori apps.

Step-by-Step: How to Design Your Financial Digital Twin

This is where many CFOs ask, “Where do we start?” Here’s a pragmatic sequence you can follow.

Step 1: Start with Business Questions, Not Tools

Clarify the decisions you want the twin to support. Examples:

  • “How exposed is our EBITDA and cash to a 10–15% demand shock in our top three markets?”
  • “Which products and customers are truly profitable after cost-to-serve?”
  • “How much working capital can we release without hurting service levels?”

Rank these questions and commit to solving the top three in your first iteration. This ensures your twin delivers visible business value quickly.

Step 2: Harmonize Data and Dimensions in S/4HANA

A digital twin fails if it rests on inconsistent structures. Work with your finance and IT teams to:

  • Standardize chart of accounts, profit centers, cost centers, and key reporting dimensions.
  • Ensure master data (customers, products, materials) is aligned across entities.
  • Confirm that the Universal Journal captures enough detail for your FP&A use cases.

This may sound “plumbing-like,” but it is foundational. Better models cannot fix broken master data.

Step 3: Build Driver Models in PaPM and CO-PA

Once the data foundation is solid, model your business logic:

  • Identify key drivers: volumes, prices, discounts, service levels, productivity, FX rates, etc.
  • Use PaPM (and, where relevant, CO-PA) to define how these drivers flow into revenues, costs, and profitability.
  • Validate the models with business owners using historical data—do they reflect reality well enough?

Aim for models that are simple enough to understand but rich enough to support decision-making.

Step 4: Implement Driver-Based Planning and Scenarios in SAC

Next, bring the twin to life for planners and business partners:

  • Build SAC planning models aligned to S/4HANA structures (no “shadow hierarchies” in Excel).
  • Replace line-item data entry with driver-based input where possible (e.g., volume and price instead of revenue by account; FTE and merit increases instead of salary lines).
  • Configure scenario and version management so FP&A can maintain base, upside, downside, and stress scenarios in parallel.

This is where business users start to “feel” the twin: they change a driver and immediately see financial impacts.

Step 5: Connect Group-Level View with Group Reporting

If you operate as a group:

  • Integrate Group Reporting with SAC so consolidated actuals flow directly into planning models.
  • Use the same structures for management and statutory views where possible to reduce reconciliation.
  • Model group scenarios: acquisitions, divestitures, segment realignments, and their impacts on leverage and covenants.

This helps your board-level discussions move from static decks to interactive, scenario-driven conversations.

Step 6: Add Process Intelligence with SAP Signavio

Once the financial twin is running, enrich it with process insights:

  • Use Signavio to analyze critical processes affecting revenue recognition, cost, and working capital (e.g., billing, collections, procurement, production).
  • Quantify how process changes (faster collections, fewer disputes, better forecast accuracy) move financial metrics.
  • Incorporate these into your PaPM and SAC models as additional levers.

Now your twin does not just answer “What if revenue drops?” but also “What if we improve our collection process by 5 days?”

Step 7: Build a Continuous Learning Loop

A digital twin should get smarter over time:

  • In SAC, set up dashboards comparing actuals vs. plan/forecast at multiple levels (entity, product, customer, segment).
  • Track forecast accuracy and bias by business area and driver.
  • Periodically recalibrate drivers and assumptions in PaPM and SAC models based on observed performance.

Over time, this turns the twin into a trusted decision-support system rather than a one-off project.

 

 

Bringing the Twin to Life: Three FP&A Use Cases

Concrete stories make the concept real for your stakeholders. Here are three you can adapt for your industry.

Use Case 1: Protecting Margins in a Demand Downturn

A global manufacturer sees early signals of softening demand:

  • S/4HANA and SAC show a drop in order intake for specific regions and products.
  • FP&A uses the twin to model a 15% volume decline and its impact on gross margin, plant utilization, and cash over the next two quarters.
  • They test combinations of levers: pricing actions, marketing cuts, production scale-backs, and working-capital measures (inventory reductions, payment-term changes).
  • Management selects a balanced response that protects strategic investments while preserving liquidity, all backed by quantified scenarios.

Instead of reactive across-the-board cuts, the digital twin enables targeted, data-driven action.

Use Case 2: Capacity Expansion and Capex Decisions

A business unit proposes a new production line:

  • PaPM models show how higher capacity and utilization would impact fixed-cost absorption and margins.
  • Group Reporting and SAC project the effect on group-level earnings, leverage, and covenants.
  • Signavio reveals that existing logistics and order-fulfillment processes are already a bottleneck; without fixing them, the new capacity would not be fully utilized.
  • The final decision bundles capex with process improvements, all tested in the twin before committing funds.

This elevates capex approvals from “business case PDFs” to interactive, scenario-based decisions.

Use Case 3: Continuous Working-Capital Steering

Working capital is often a major, but under-optimized, source of cash:

  • The twin brings together DSO, DPO, inventory days, and order patterns from S/4HANA.
  • SAC running predictive models and driver-based forecasts shows future cash conversion under different collection, payment, and inventory strategies.
  • Treasury and FP&A jointly manage liquidity by simulating changes to terms, discount programs, and inventory policies.

Here, the digital twin becomes a daily steering tool, not just a quarterly working-capital report.

How to Talk About This Within Your Organization

For CFOs and FP&A leaders, positioning is key. A few suggestions:

  • With the board:  Emphasize resilience, speed of response, and better capital allocation, not system features.
  • With business leaders: Focus on usable scenarios, transparency on drivers, and the ability to test decisions safely before executing.
  • With IT and SAP teams: Frame it as an architecture and data challenge tied to specific business outcomes, not just another reporting project.

You are not “installing a tool”; you are building a new way for your organization to see itself and make decisions.

Final Thought: FP&A as the Architect of the Financial Digital Twin

The digital twin is a strategic opportunity for FP&A to step up as the architect of performance, not just the reporter of results. SAP gives you the building blocks, but only finance can define the questions, drivers, and scenarios that matter.

If you’re starting this journey, pick one or two high-impact use cases, assemble the right SAP components around them, and prove the value quickly. From there, your financial digital twin can expand step by step—until it becomes the default way your company plans, decides, and acts.

 

Feel free to connect—I'm excited to collaborate and help your team unlock the full potential of financial planning!

entry.title: Issue with ANS HTML mail Template
entry.content:
Hello,

We are currently using Alert Notification Service (ANS) to send exception email alerts.

We have a requirement to display the alert details in an HTML table format. From SAP Automation Pilot, we are triggering the SendANSEvent command and passing the HTML table content in the event body. Additionally, the “Use HTML” option has been enabled in the email action, and we are using a custom SMTP configuration.

However, despite enabling HTML, the email is still rendering the HTML table as plain text, and the table tags are visible instead of being formatted properly.

Could you please help us understand why the HTML content is not being rendered correctly and advise on the correct approach to achieve this requirement?

Looking forward to your guidance.

Best regards,
Asif Rajekhan

Thanks

Asif

entry.title: Re: SAP to Microsoft Excel Integration for trial balance
entry.content:
Dear @AmitKalaskar,

For Public Cloud the standard API you want for a Trial Balance extract is:

  • Trial Balance Read (OData V2), Technical name C_TrialBalance_CDS
    It returns starting/debit/credit/ending balances for G/L accounts
  • It is delivered under the communication scenario Finance Accounting Analytics Integration (SAP_COM_0303)

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/61ab22d2f6da49a394004e459947e994.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/5792333ddf3c47eaad4314b071dfd684.html

Step 1.

A. Create a Communication User

1. App Communication Users ->New

2. Create user + password

B. Create a Communication System

1. App Communication Systems ->New

2. Maintain a system name/id

3. In Users for Inbound Communication assign the Communication User you created

C. Create a Communication Arrangement

1. App Communication Arrangements ->New

2. Choose communication scenario SAP_COM_0303

 

3. Select your Communication System

4. Look for the inbound service Trial Balance Read / C_TrialBalance_CDS

5. Copy the service URL shown in the inbound services to use this host for Excel

Step 2.

This API is commonly consumed via the function import + /Results with mandatory PostingDate From/To parameters plus filters and a $select otherwise it can time out

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3588269

Why this matters:

  • Provide a filter at least Ledger + CompanyCode
  • Use $select / aggregation level to avoid timeouts
  • Too many selected fields or too wide ranges can trigger backend DB errors

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3689885

Step 3.

Follow the steps from the blog mentioned in your question:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/s-4hana-cloud-integration-microsoft-excel/bc-p/13387926

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
In this document, I will go through the architecture and benefits of HANA DataLake Files service in the BDC Datasphere application. In the SAP Business business Data cloud there are 2 instances of HDLF object stores,

  1. Foundation services HDLF object store - managed by SAP
  2. Datasphere  HDLF object store - managed by Customers

Object store is a file storage from Hyperscalers like Amazon s3 to store large amounts of data files(csv, parquet and delta tables)

FOS HDLF object store is used for SAP standard data products, when an intelligent application is activated in BDC Cockpit, required standard data products are installed and data is loaded to FOS HDLF object store. these Data products are available as Delta tables in the FOS HDLF, can be shared with Datasphere HANA Space or Databricks for data analytics and data science models. FOS HDLF is managed by SAP, Customers don't have access to it. 

Datasphere HDLF object store is used for storing large data files from SAP and non SAP systems. these files are available as Delta tables. Customers can configure the ETL to replicate and transform data into Datasphere HDLF space. Also, custom data products can be generated in the Datasphere HDLF from BW PCE in BDC. 

In this article, we focus on the Datasphere HDLF object store.

HDLF object store was made available in Standalone Datasphere(BTP) as part of data fabric solution from SAP, later it is introduced as part of BDC.

Benefits:

  • HDLF object store is cost effective solution,  1TB is 0.1 CU per hour.
  • Spark compute for delta table creation, scalable runtime for transformation flows in the HDLF space.
  • "SQL on files" feature, read data from HDLF space into HANA cloud models for analytics.

You can check the price quote for the object store and compute in the BDC price estimator https://bdc-pricing-estimator-sac-sacus10.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/

Architecture:

  • Embedded HDLF object store in BDC Datasphere is optional.
  • It requires additional capacity units, BDC pricing estimator can provide the number.
  • Data is replicated from Source systems and stored as Delta tables using Replication flows.
  • Delta tables are parquet files with transaction logs and maintained in the HDLF object store.
  • SQL on Files engine can read data from Delta tables of HDLF object store in HANA cloud without loading them to HANA Database. It is a Native HANA feature and explained in detail in this SAP blog SQL on Files 
  • Apache SPARK compute provides scalable runtime for the transformation flows to process delta table(Parquet files) from Bronze to Silver to Gold layer Delta tables(Medallion architecture).

Configuration of Datasphere object store

The minimum capacity units required to activate Object Store in BDC is 4150.05 CU (128 GB of SAP HANA memory). You can Simulate price the BDC estimator, steps are provided in the SAP blog Objectstore CUs

After that you can configure the Tenant in Datasphere --> System --> Configuration --> Tenant configuration and add the Object store units. You can find the detail steps in the SAP blog Configuring the Tenant

after that, when we create a new space in Datasphere, option to choose HANA cloud or HDLF space will be enabled.

HANA Space Vs HDLF Space in Datasphere:

After configuring HDLF object store, we have 2 spaces in Datasphere.

  • HANA space
  • HANA DataLake file space. 

HANA cloud space is the traditional space available in the Standalone Datasphere tenant(BTP), it requires HANA memory and HANA compute engine to store and process the data. On the other side, HDLF space is available in the BDC Datasphere and uses SPARK compute and object storage.

we must choose the space that we intend to work and build the objects accordingly. monitoring of jobs are specific to the space. Overall the look and feel experience for object development and monitoring jobs is same in both spaces.

In the HDLF space, replication flows and transformation flows are used to replicate and transform the large data files. Modeling objects like Graphical or SQL views and analytic models can't be developed in this space.

We can share the HDLF local delta tables(files) in HANA space and use them in transformation flows and Vice versa. that is Share the HANA space Local tables in a HDLF space and use them transformation flows.

End to end process flows Replication flow + Transformation flows

Replication flows are used to integrate/replicate data from Source systems into Inbound buffer first, then a merge task needs to be executed to write data to Delta table file. Transformation flows are used to transform the data to silver and gold layers of Delta table files in HDLF object store. These Delta tables can be shared in HANA space of Datasphere in the view builder.

Importance of merge task:

Since the Local tables (files) contains large data volumes, merge task updates the data by allocating required amount of SPARK compute resources. You can enable the merge task directly in the Replication flow configuration or manually run it from the monitor. You can schedule it as a task in the Task chains. Now you can cancel a running merge task in the monitor tab.  Merge task is to be required for BW Data product generator delta table files also.

Note: In case of merge task is not run, then data is not available for reporting.

Python script can be written in the transformation flows and run with SPARK compute to process large data files.

Data product generator for Object store in Datasphere:

Data products are created as Local Delta tables(files) in the object store of Datasphere from BW InfoProviders. For the Subscriptions that are created in BW PCE, even delta data can be pushed from BW ADSO into Object store. these delta local tables can be shared in other applications.

Source from SAP

More detail explanation on the BW data products can be found this blog Data Product Generator

Limitations of Object store in Datasphere:

  • Replication Flows currently do not support local table (files) as source. So only Inbound.
  • Only 5 HDLF spaces can be created in Datasphere tenant.
  • Objects without Primary key cannot be loaded into embedded object store
  • Support of local type “initial load” only in Replication Flows is not supported.
  • Some datatypes don't support in Replication flows of HDLF space.
  • You will need to check the connection documentation to make sure if it is supported for Data lake files or not.

Summary: SAP Datasphere object store is a cost effective solution to store large data files as delta tables and transform the data with SPARK runtime and share them as data products in SAP Databricks.

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA High Availability - Technical Scope, Mechanisms, and Design Boundaries
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA provides a set of explicitly documented mechanisms to support high availability (HA) in productive landscapes. These mechanisms are designed to minimize unplanned downtime, enable fast continuation of operations, and preserve transactional consistency when specific failure types occur.

High availability in SAP HANA is not a single feature. It is an architecture composed of multiple building blocks, each with a clearly documented technical objective, scope, and boundary. These building blocks are described across the SAP HANA Administration Guide and related SAP Help Portal documentation.

This article consolidates SAP-documented SAP HANA high-availability mechanisms into a single technical view.
Its purpose is to make scope, interaction, and design boundaries explicit, enabling technically sound architecture design, realistic availability expectations, and precise discussions.

All statements are derived from official SAP documentation.

1. High Availability in SAP HANA - Technical Definition
From a technical perspective, high availability in SAP HANA focuses on:

  • minimizing unplanned downtime
  • enabling fast system continuation after certain failure types
  • preserving transactional consistency during failover or takeover

High availability is explicitly distinct from:

  • backup and recovery, which restore data to a previous state
  • disaster recovery, which addresses site-level or regional failures

This separation of objectives is consistently reflected in SAP HANA documentation and tooling.

Key boundary
High availability mechanisms maintain continuity of operation, not historical restore points.

2. SAP HANA High Availability Building Blocks
SAP HANA high availability is achieved by combining several documented mechanisms:

  • Host Auto-Failover
  • SAP HANA System Replication
  • Scale-Up and Scale-Out topologies
  • SAP-supported cluster frameworks on certified operating systems (for example, Pacemaker)

Each mechanism addresses specific failure classes and operates within a defined technical scope. No single mechanism covers all failure scenarios.

3. Host Auto-Failover - Technical Scope and Boundaries
3.1 Purpose and Mechanism
Host Auto-Failover is designed to handle host-level failures within a SAP HANA landscape.
In supported configurations:

  • a standby host is configured
  • SAP HANA detects a host failure
  • database services are automatically reassigned to the standby host

This reassignment is performed by SAP HANA itself based on internal monitoring and configuration.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA Host Auto-Failover

3.2 Technical Objective
Host Auto-Failover focuses on:

  • host failure detection
  • service reassignment
  • automatic continuation of operations

It does not:

  • provide historical restore points
  • protect against logical data errors
  • replace backup or recovery mechanisms

Key boundary
Host Auto-Failover preserves service execution, not data history.

4. SAP HANA System Replication in the HA Context
4.1 Technical Role
SAP HANA System Replication (SR) is a core building block for high availability and disaster tolerance scenarios.
In the HA context, SR:

  • continuously transfers data changes from a primary system to a secondary system
  • enables fast takeover in case of primary system failure
  • preserves transactional consistency at takeover time

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Overview

4.2 Replication Modes and Availability Behavior
SAP HANA System Replication supports different replication modes, such as synchronous and asynchronous replication.
From a high-availability perspective:

  • the replication mode defines when changes are acknowledged
  • the secondary system reflects the current state of the primary system

Logical changes, deletions, or corruptions on the primary system are replicated as part of normal operation.
This behavior is inherent to replication and aligns with the documented objective of SR as an availability mechanism.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Replication Modes

Key boundary
System replication maintains current state, not historical restore points.

5. Cluster Management and Pacemaker – Technical Role
5.1 Purpose of Cluster Frameworks
In supported Linux-based SAP HANA environments, cluster frameworks such as Pacemaker are used to orchestrate:

  • system and service monitoring
  • failover and takeover actions
  • role coordination between primary and secondary systems

Pacemaker does not replace SAP HANA availability mechanisms.
It coordinates and automates actions around them.

SAP HANA System Replication with Cluster Management (Pacemaker)
(Implementation is covered by OS-vendor specific best-practice guides, not by a single SAP HANA Platform help page.)

5.2 Design Boundary
From a technical perspective:

  • Pacemaker does not replicate data
  • Pacemaker does not provide restore points
  • Pacemaker does not replace SAP HANA logic

It acts as an orchestration layer for documented HA mechanisms.

6. Failure Classes and HA Coverage
SAP HANA high-availability mechanisms address specific failure classes:

  • Host failures
    handled by Host Auto-Failover and cluster-managed restarts
  • Primary system failures
    handled by system replication and takeover
  • Service-level failures
    handled by internal SAP HANA restart logic and cluster coordination

Coverage depends on:

  • architecture
  • topology (scale-up vs. scale-out)
  • configuration

No single mechanism covers all failure types.

7. High Availability vs. Recovery and Disaster Recovery
From a technical standpoint:

  • High availability minimizes downtime
  • Backup and recovery restore data to a previous state
  • Disaster recovery handles site-level failure domains

Although system replication may appear in both HA and DR designs, objectives, assumptions, and validation criteria differ.

High availability focuses on:

  • continuity
  • fast takeover
  • minimal disruption

Recovery and DR focus on:

  • reconstruction
  • historical restore points
  • controlled recovery after broader failures

8. Design Boundaries and Architectural Implications
From an architecture perspective:

  • high availability is achieved through combination, not a single feature
  • each HA mechanism has explicit limits
  • realistic availability expectations require alignment between:
    • failure scenarios
    • documented mechanism scope
    • operational readiness

Understanding these boundaries is essential for technically sound SAP HANA availability design.

Summary
SAP HANA provides a comprehensive, well-documented set of high-availability mechanisms.

High availability is not defined by a single feature, but by the interaction of multiple documented components, each addressing specific failure scenarios within defined technical boundaries.

By understanding the scope, mechanisms, and limits of Host Auto-Failover, System Replication, and cluster-based coordination, SAP HANA landscapes can be designed with clear, realistic availability expectations.

entry.title: Extending Custom RAP App
entry.content:
Hello SAP RAP experts,

I am working on a custom RAP application and I have two requirements I want to implement. I would like your advice on whether they are feasible, especially considering that I only have Web IDE access and not BAS.

Requirement 1 :

Add a Send Mail button on the main page of the app.

The button should not send the email immediately. Instead, it should open a popup/dialog showing an editable email template with: Subject - Sender - Body - Attachments ..., the user can then either click Send to send the email or cancel to discard it.

Requirement 2:

Add an action button (or icon) in each line of the table (line item), when clicked, the user should see a list of attachments related to the PO for that line, and be able to open/view them.

My main question: I know that something like this might be done using a UI5 extension, but all examples I have found require BAS. Since I only have Web IDE access, I’m not sure if it’s possible to implement these enhancements.

I would really appreciate any guidance, suggestions, or best practices for this scenario.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Delivery Locked on Auto Invoice via BAPI
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

We have a business requirement to automatically create an invoice when posting a goods issue in VL02N.

The invoice creation itself works fine. However, when we try to link it to the delivery flow via a BAPI, the system returns 'Delivery Locked'.

This seems expected because a customized output type (with a special function type) is triggered immediately when posting the goods issue. At that moment, the standard system behavior temporarily locks the delivery, preventing the BAPI from updating the delivery document flow with the invoice.

This issue is blocking my process. Any guidance, best practices, or workarounds to handle this scenario would be highly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: 🤔 Is This the Right Way to Build Talent Intelligent Hub Integration with SAP Gene AI Hub-Extension
entry.content:
****Please check design at the bottom of this article (available for download)***

I've been sketching out an architecture for extending SAP SuccessFactors with AI-powered skills matching and requisition management. But before going down this path, I want to challenge my own assumptions. Is this the best approach, or am I overengineering?

THE PROPOSED ARCHITECTURE:

Here's what I'm considering:

🏗️ Multi-Cloud Foundation (SAP BTP)

- Cloud Foundry runtime for microservices

- HANA DB for data persistence

- Integration Suite for SuccessFactors connectivity

- Identity services for authentication

🤖AI & Analytics Layer

- SAP Generative AI Hub (GPT-4, Claude, Llama)

- Document intelligence and entity extraction

- Vector embeddings for semantic search

- Resume parsing and skills extraction

⚙️Microservices Architecture

- Requisition Analysis Service

- Skills Management Service

- Matching Service

- Custom build services for each capability

📊Advanced Data Layer

- Vector engine for embeddings

- Object store for documents

- HANA DB for structured data

- Build Process Automation for workflows

THE CRITICAL QUESTIONS I'M WRESTLING WITH:

1️⃣DO WE REALLY NEED MULTI-CLOUD COMPLEXITY?

The Good:

✅Flexibility to choose best-of-breed services

✅Avoid vendor lock-in

✅Leverage SAP BTP's enterprise capabilities

The Concerns:

⚠️Operational complexity across multiple clouds

⚠️Higher costs for integration and management

⚠️Steeper learning curve for teams

⚠️More points of failure

Alternative: Could we start with pure SuccessFactors extensions and only move to BTP if we hit limitations?

2️⃣IS MICROSERVICES THE RIGHT PATTERN HERE?

The Good:

✅Independent scaling of services

✅Team autonomy and faster deployment

✅Technology flexibility per service

The Concerns:

⚠️Distributed system complexity

⚠️Network latency between services

⚠️Harder to debug and monitor

⚠️Overkill for a small team or MVP?

Alternative: What if we started with a modular monolith and decomposed later based on actual bottlenecks?

3️⃣ARE WE READY FOR GENERATIVE AI IN PRODUCTION HR?

The Good:

✅Intelligent resume parsing and skills extraction

✅Natural language requisition analysis

✅Semantic matching beyond keyword search

✅Document generation and summarization

The Concerns:

⚠️Hallucination risks in HR decisions

⚠️Bias and fairness implications

⚠️Explainability requirements for hiring

⚠️Cost per API call at scale

⚠️Data privacy and compliance (GDPR, EEOC)

Alternative: Should we start with rule-based matching and traditional NLP, then layer in GenAI for non-critical features?

4️⃣DO WE NEED VECTOR EMBEDDINGS AND SEMANTIC SEARCH?

The Good:

✅Find similar skills even with different terminology

✅Semantic matching beyond exact keywords

✅Better candidate-requisition alignment

The Concerns:

⚠️Added infrastructure complexity (vector DB)

⚠️Embedding model maintenance and updates

⚠️Explainability: "Why was this candidate matched?"

⚠️Cost of generating and storing embeddings

Alternative: Could traditional search with synonyms and taxonomies get us 80% of the value with 20% of the complexity?

5️⃣IS THE INTEGRATION LAYER OVER-ARCHITECTED?

The Good:

✅Clean separation of concerns

✅Reusable integration patterns

✅API management and event mesh

The Concerns:

⚠️Do we need event mesh for this use case?

⚠️Is Build Process Automation necessary or could we use simpler workflows?

⚠️Are we adding layers that slow down development?

Alternative: Direct SuccessFactors OData APIs with simple retry logic?

WHAT MIGHT BE A SIMPLER STARTING POINT?

Phase 1: Validate the Value

- Build a SuccessFactors extension app (UI5)

- Use SuccessFactors APIs directly

- Simple keyword-based matching

- Manual review and approval workflows

- Prove the business value first

Phase 2: Add Intelligence Gradually

- Introduce resume parsing (traditional NLP)

- Add skills taxonomy and synonyms

- Basic analytics dashboard

- Measure improvement over baseline

Phase 3: Scale What Works

- Only then consider microservices if we have clear scaling needs

- Add GenAI for specific high-value use cases

- Introduce vector search if keyword matching proves insufficient

- Expand to multi-cloud only if we need capabilities not in SuccessFactors

THE REAL QUESTIONS:

💭Am I solving for scale we don't have yet?

💭Am I choosing technology because it's interesting vs. because it's necessary?

💭What's the simplest thing that could possibly work?

💭How do we balance innovation with pragmatism?

💭What would a true MVP look like?

WHAT I'D LOVE YOUR INPUT ON:

🔹Have you built similar HR/talent extensions? What worked? What didn't?

🔹Where have you seen GenAI add real value in recruiting vs. just hype?

🔹When is microservices worth it vs. premature optimization?

🔹How do you balance "enterprise-grade" with "ship fast and learn"?

🔹What would you do differently?

I'm genuinely torn between building something robust and future-proof vs. starting simple and evolving based on real needs.

What's your take? Is this architecture the right approach, or should we start simpler and earn our way to complexity?

Drop your thoughts below - especially if you think I'm overcomplicating this! 👇

#EnterpriseArchitecture #SAPSuccessFactors #SAPBTP #TalentManagement #AIinHR #Microservices #CloudArchitecture #SoftwareEngineering #TechLeadership #ArchitecturalDecisions #BuildOrBuy #MVPFirst #OverengineeringDebate

entry.title: Re: ServiceNow Incident to CALM Defect - Incident Management
entry.content: Yes, this is possible, but it typically requires integration rather than a native feature. Use SAP Integration Suite or a middleware layer to connect ServiceNow with SAP Cloud ALM via APIs. When an incident is created in ServiceNow, trigger an API call to automatically create a defect in CALM and map fields like description, priority, and assignment. For Hypercare, many teams implement this using event based triggers or webhooks to avoid manual tracking. Check the Cloud ALM API documentation for defect creation endpoints, as it can serve as the written reference for your customer.

entry.title: Re: SAP CPQ → Tableau?
entry.content:
@smorales01 

The same principles apply as with Tableau.

While REST APIs  can be used to expose CPQ data to Tableau or Power BI, in larger environments it is generally advisable to avoid direct real-time BI calls to CPQ.

A more stable pattern is to extract data via APIs into an intermediate layer (e.g. CPI, a data warehouse, or scheduled ETL) and let the BI tool consume it from there. This improves performance, governance, and refresh control as data volumes grow.

entry.title: SPAU cannot change status Partly obsolete, completely implemented, has to be confirmed

As result, after upgrade we can see minimum delta in SPAU (between DEV-QA), but one of SAP note is cannot be confirmed/adjusted. It has status "Partly obsolete, completely implemented, has to be confirmed".

entry.title: SAP S/4 HANA OData v4 APIs available - Quick Reference and available in SAP API Business Hub
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA exposes a rich catalog of OData V4 APIs on the SAP API Business Hub, enabling modern, lightweight, and efficient integration. These APIs support RESTful access with improved performance, reduced payload sizes (JSON), and advanced metadata modeling. You can explore and test all available OData V4 APIs—both for S/4HANA On‑Premise —directly from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Administration Notes

SAP provides two key transactions for OData V4 lifecycle:

  • /IWBEP/V4_ADMIN → Service registration in backend
  • /IWFND/V4_ADMIN → Service group publishing on Gateway hub
These ensure proper activation before consumption in tools like SAPUI5, SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Integration Suite,  Postman, or custom integrations.

Where to Find OData V4 APIs (363 Objects - as of 15th Feb 2026)

All S/4HANA OData V4 services are available on:

ODATA V4 API : https://api.sap.com/package/S4HANAOPAPI/odatav4 

NameTypeDisplayNameSubTypeVersionDescription
sap-s4-OP_PPDSPRODTDSTLV_0001-v1APIPPDS Product Time Dependent Stock LevelODATAV41.0.0Create, read, update, and adjust intervals in Planning Version Dependent Time-Dependent Stock Levels (TDSL) for Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling (PP/DS)

entry.title: Re: SAP CPQ Set Product Price dynamically from attribute...
entry.content:
Hi @vromeo30 ,

 

Based on the screenshots, the Test value is correctly entered on the configurable product. However, the pricing formula <*VALUE(Test)*> is evaluated in the context of the referenced simple product, which does not automatically receive the configurable product's value, as it does not share the same attribute context.

Value inheritance in SAP CPQ is supported within a true parent/child bundle structure between configurable items. In your case, you are referencing a simple product inside a configurable product, and that setup does not provide automatic attribute value inheritance into the simple product's pricing context.

 

The simple product evaluates its own attribute state. Since its Test attribute is not populated at pricing time, the formula returns 0. While simple products can have attributes (including preselected values), they do not automatically inherit attribute values from the configurable product.

In this scenario, the most reliable solution is to use scripting (e.g. On Product Complete, On Rule Execution End, or a Change Trigger on the Test attribute) to explicitly assign the simple product's price based on the configurable product's Test value. This ensures deterministic pricing behaviour rather than relying on implicit propagation.

Hope this clarifies the behaviour.

entry.title: Re: Display configuration values by user login language i...
entry.content:
Hi @burcu_kumlar ,

In addition to the language-dependent configuration settings already mentioned, and assuming that all attribute value translations are properly maintained in the system (a prerequisite for the translation engine to function correctly), there are two important options in Document Generation -> General Settings that should be verified.

 

First, ensure that "Translate quotes using the user's default dictionary" is enabled. This setting allows the system to generate documents based on the logged-in user's dictionary, ensuring that configuration values are rendered in the appropriate language where translations are available.

Second, consider enabling "Users can choose customer's language when creating documents". This provides flexibility during document generation by allowing the user to explicitly select the output language. This is particularly useful when the customer's primary language differs from the user's login language.

 

When both settings are correctly configured, SAP CPQ will resolve configuration values according to the selected language context during document generation, ensuring controlled and predictable multilingual document output in Quote 2.0

entry.title: The Future of SAP ABAP Based Systems with AI: Why MCP Servers Matter
entry.content:
What if your AI assistant could actually talk to your SAP system?

Not through screenshots. Not through copy-paste. But real, direct communication where you ask "Help me find and analyze all enhancements related to Sales Process" or "Give me an RCA document for the ABAP dump I saw in production" and get an instant answer pulled straight from your SAP environment.

This isn't science fiction. It's happening now, thanks to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.

The Problem We've All Been Ignoring

Let's be honest: for all the hype about AI transforming enterprise software, most of us are still using ChatGPT and Claude like glorified search engines. We describe our SAP problems, get generic advice, then manually dig through transactions to find what we need.

The disconnect is painful. AI assistants are incredibly smart, but they're blind to our actual code. They can't see your complex pricing procedures, can't check your vendor master data, can't analyze your cash flow in real-time.

Until now.

Enter MCP: The Missing Link

To understand why MCP matters, let's take a step back and look at how we've traditionally communicated with SAP systems.

The Evolution of SAP Communication

In the beginning, there was DIAG (Dynamic Information and Action Gateway) the proprietary protocol that allowed SAP GUI to communicate with SAP servers. It worked, but it was closed, SAP specific, and limited to the GUI client. If you wanted to interact with SAP, you needed SAP GUI.

Then came the shift toward modern development tools. SAP introduced the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) framework - the foundation that powers ABAP in Eclipse. ADT brought a crucial innovation: a standardized way for external tools to communicate with SAP systems over HTTP/HTTPS, exposing SAP functionality through REST-like APIs.

ADT framework opened doors:

 

  • Eclipse could retrieve code, metadata, and system information
  • External tools could trigger ABAP unit tests, syntax checks, and activations
  • Developers got modern IDE features while working with SAP systems

 

And this is exactly what Vibing Steampunk by Alice Vinogradova leverages.

The MCP server uses the same ADT framework that Eclipse uses to communicate with SAP. It's not reinventing the wheel. it's standing on SAP's own modern integration foundation.

But Here's Where MCP Takes It Further

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI assistants connect directly to external systems. Think of it as a universal translator between AI and enterprise applications.

While ADT framework solved the "how to communicate with SAP" problem, MCP solves the "how to make AI understand and interact with SAP" problem.

Instead of you being the middleman between AI and SAP, the AI can:

 

  • Query SAP system metadata through ADT endpoints
  • Interpret the results in business context
  • Give you actionable insights in natural language

 

The combination is powerful: ADT provides the technical connectivity, MCP provides the intelligent interface.

Real Example: Vibing Steampunk MCP Server

I recently discovered (and have been experimenting with) a project called Vibing Steampunk on GitHub. It's an MCP server specifically built for communicating with ABAP-based systems.

But here's where it gets interesting for developers: this isn't just about querying data. It's about how AI can help you build and understand SAP systems.

Scenario: Understanding a BAPI

Before MCP:

 

  • You open SE37 → Search for BAPIs → Read documentation → Check parameters → Test in SE37
  • Time: 20-30 minutes of navigation and documentation reading

 

With MCP:

 

  • You (to AI): "Show me the BAPI parameters for creating outgoing payments and explain the mandatory fields"
  • AI queries SAP directly → Retrieves BAPI metadata → Explains in context
  • Time: 30 seconds

 

Scenario: Code Analysis

Before MCP:

 

  • You inherit a custom payment program
  • You trace through ABAP code → Check database tables → Understand data flow → Document logic
  • Time: Hours or days depending on complexity

 

With MCP:

 

  • You: "Analyze this payment posting program and explain the data flow from BAPI_XXXXXX to the database tables"
  • AI queries SAP for BAPI structure → Examines table relationships → Maps the data flow → Explains in plain language
  • Time: Minutes

 

Why This Matters for ABAP Developers

1. Faster Onboarding

MCP enabled AI guides new developers through your SAP landscape showing payment BAPI locations, custom table structures, and organizational extensions dramatically reducing the learning curve.

2. Living Documentation

Forget stale documents. AI queries your actual SAP system for real-time information on BAPI signatures, custom table relationships, function module usage, and data element definitions.

3. Intelligent Code Assistance

Beyond GitHub Copilot, AI understands your environment:

 

  • "Show payment blocks in REGUH"
  • "Explain BSEG-payment document relationships"
  • "Find programs calling BAPI_XXXXXX"

 

It queries your system for accurate, environment-specific answers.

4. Faster Debugging

Skip manual tracing through BAPI dumps, SLG1 logs, and database states. Ask "Why did payment 12345 fail?" and AI retrieves error logs, checks document structure, and explains the issue.

5. Knowledge Transfer

Preserve senior developer expertise. MCP enabled AI explains custom code rationale, shows configuration context, and guides juniors through complex payment scenarios using real system data.

Important: The AI never executes code directly in SAP it requests information through the MCP server, which enforces all authorization checks and logs every action.

What This Means for the Industry

We're at an inflection point. SAP has announced Joule, their AI copilot. Microsoft has Copilot integrated across Dynamics and Power Platform. Oracle has embedded AI across their cloud suite.

But here's what makes MCP different: it's open and extensible.

You're not locked into one vendor's AI. You can:

 

  • Use the AI assistant you prefer (Claude, ChatGPT, local models)
  • Customize the integration for your specific business processes
  • Build proprietary MCP servers for competitive advantage
  • Integrate multiple systems (SAP + Salesforce + your data warehouse)

 

The Bigger Picture for Development Teams

This isn't just about making development faster. It's about fundamentally changing how we build and maintain SAP systems.

Imagine:

Onboarding: New ABAP developers asking "How does our payment posting process work?" and getting a complete explanation with actual code references and data flows from your system

Code Reviews: AI assistant examining your BAPI wrapper code and saying "This won't work for payment method 'C' because field REGUH-RZALW is mandatory but not populated"

Impact Analysis: Before modifying a payment table structure, asking "What custom programs will be affected by changing table ZPAYMENT_LOG?" and getting a complete dependency map

Documentation: AI automatically generating technical specs by analyzing your actual implementation, not from templates

Troubleshooting: During production issues, asking "Show me all failed payment postings in the last hour with their error messages" and getting analyzed results with suggested fixes

We're moving from "AI that knows ABAP syntax" to "AI that understands YOUR SAP system."

That's not a minor upgrade. That's a paradigm shift in how we develop, maintain, and transfer knowledge in SAP environments.

Your Turn

Have you experimented with connecting AI assistants to your SAP development environment? What repetitive tasks would you want AI to help with? What concerns do you have about AI accessing system metadata?

I'm genuinely curious to hear from the ABAP community. The technology is ready. The question is: what development challenges will we solve with it?

Interested in the technical implementation? Check out the Vibing Steampunk MCP server on GitHub and let me know what you think. Let's build the future of SAP development together.

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
Great blog post @SreekanthSurampally - think it's quite important to have articles on the Object Store architecture, given its novelty in the overall Datasphere/BDC.

Think that there is still quite a lot confusion related to the Object Store vs. more 'older' HANA Data Lake (relational one), and which one should be used, for what exactly, how much they cost compared to each other and compared to the traditional HANA Cloud 'disk' storage etc.

Yet, what I still cannot fully comprehend - Object Store (HANA Data Lake on Files) costs roughly ~0.075 CU per GB-Month, while traditional HANA Cloud disk costs roughly ~0.18 CU per GB-Month (OK, the former requires also some compute and requests in addition, while the latter requires memory/compute). It's (very roughly, if we also disregard compression etc.) x2.4 cost rationing, and not say x10 rationing (that one might expect), for a non-negligible performance and modelling overhead.

To put things into perspective, in the same manner, the 'older' (relational engine) HANA Data Lake storage costs roughly ~0.56 CU per GB-Month (OK, compute included), which then makes it even more expensive than the HANA Cloud disk storage - cannot see then why would it be even ever used if more expensive and slower and more complex(?!)

Or maybe I'm missing some important points in reading the whole thing? 🙂

entry.title: Re: C_IEE2E Practical Exam Under Review → Attempt Closed...
entry.content:
Hi Taofiksanni, unfortunately I cannot answer your question directly but will share my experience with some technical issues I faced in my exam last Friday the 13th of Feb 26 on the Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting exam. 

First of all, I didn't have access to CBC, neither via a specific link and via "manage your solution" app, once clicked I got an "unauthorized" error in the new window, probably because of misconfiguration in IAS groups for CBC or login info. 

Secondly, the exam asks you to create the journal entries and documents with a reference like P###, swapping '###' for the number you're assigned, but this explanation if VERY poorly given, the only number I got was my login as 'F123456' and SAP doesn't give a clue if you need to swap 123 or 456 into this reference. Also, some questions ask you to create some objects (BPs, Cost Centers) with this naming convention, but some objects already exist, leaving you with no clue if you can use them or not.

The overall result was that I lost 30 minutes in the beginning of the exam and did my whole exam without being sure if I was submitting my answers correctly, I was quite confident with my answers but scored only 4% and obviously failed. I strongly believe the review system couldn't validate my answers. 

I opened a ticket to the support and will update everyone here. I hope to get some answers and get my attempt credit back and share my experience with other people who is about to take the same FICO exams in the future.

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
Really appreciate the depth and clarity in this write-up. The way you explained the Object Store architecture within SAP Datasphere and its positioning inside SAP Business Data Cloud makes it much easier to understand where it fits in real project scenarios.

 

The breakdown of benefits — especially around cost optimization, scalability with Spark processing, and medallion-style layering — was very practical. This is particularly relevant for customers handling large historical datasets or data product scenarios. Now 1 Question.

1. In hybrid architectures where some datasets remain in in-memory HANA tables for performance, how do you typically decide what should stay in the Object Store vs. what should be modeled in core Datasphere tables? Are there practical sizing or latency benchmarks you recommend?

entry.title: India : Practical use inquiry on the "Invoice Reduction" function
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

Do you use the “Invoice Reduction” function in Create Supplier Invoice / MIRO in India?

I have also consulted Joule for Consultants; however, I am not in a position to confirm whether the responses fully reflect actual business practice. I would greatly appreciate your insights based on real project or customer experience in India, or a Joule-based answer that has been validated in practice.

Our intention is to understand the actual practice in India.

The background of this question relates to the “Invoice Reduction” function available during supplier invoice creation in SAP. We would like to understand whether this function is commonly used in India and whether it is typically applicable for customers there.

For reference, in Japan this function is generally not used. When there is a price difference at the time of invoicing, it is typically handled by either:

  • correcting the PO price, or using subsequent debit/credit functionality,

rather than directly reducing the invoice amount via the invoice reduction function.

Could you kindly share the common practice in India based on your experience?

Thank you very much in advance for your guidance.

entry.title: Re: Unable to add email domain
entry.content: As per KBA 3025172 - How to add/remove email domains - SAP for Me, only Super Administrators and Cloud Administrators can use the Maintain Allowed Company Domains application to register and maintain a list of official Internet domains for their company. Since you are neither a super admin nor a cloud admin, this application  Maintain Allowed Company Domains is not accessible for you. For data security reason, our SAP always recommend customer to manage the domain by themselves. Thus please contact below super admin or cloud admin to help add the domain.

entry.title: El reto real del CFO actual
entry.content:
La mayoría de las organizaciones aún operan con procesos financieros que:

  • Dependen de reconciliaciones manuales
  • Tienen información fragmentada entre FI, CO y módulos satélite
  • Cierran tarde y con alta presión operativa
  • Reaccionan, en lugar de anticipar

El problema no es el equipo. Es la arquitectura financiera heredada.

SAP S/4HANA: Finanzas en tiempo real, no al cierre del mes

SAP S/4HANA redefine el rol de Finanzas a partir de un concepto clave: el Universal Journal.

Esto permite:

  • Un solo registro financiero (ACDOCA)
  • Eliminación de reconciliaciones entre FI y CO
  • Información consistente desde el origen
  • Reportes en tiempo real, sin esperar al cierre

Para el CFO, esto se traduce en algo muy concreto: confianza en los números, en cualquier momento.

Cierre financiero más rápido, con menos esfuerzo

Uno de los mayores dolores del CFO es el cierre mensual.

Con SAP S/4HANA:

  • Se automatizan tareas repetitivas
  • Se reducen ajustes de último minuto
  • El cierre deja de ser un evento traumático

El beneficio no es solo cerrar más rápido, sino liberar tiempo del equipo financiero para análisis y estrategia.

De control operativo a visión estratégica

SAP S/4HANA permite a Finanzas:

  • Analizar rentabilidad en tiempo real
  • Monitorear flujo de efectivo de forma predictiva
  • Detectar desviaciones antes de que se conviertan en problemas
  • Integrar analítica directamente en los procesos (Embedded Analytics)

Esto cambia la conversación del CFO con la Dirección General: de explicar resultados a guiar decisiones.

Automatización e inteligencia: menos operación, más valor

S/4HANA, combinado con soluciones como SAP BTP, IA y herramientas de automatización, habilita:

  • Conciliaciones automáticas
  • Cash Application inteligente
  • Detección de anomalías
  • Forecast financiero más preciso

No se trata de reemplazar personas, sino de elevar el rol del talento financiero.

Cloud, control y escalabilidad

Para muchos CFOs, la pregunta no es si migrar, sino cómo hacerlo sin perder control.

SAP S/4HANA ofrece:

  • Modelos Cloud y On-Premise
  • Seguridad y cumplimiento normativo
  • Escalabilidad para crecer sin rediseñar Finanzas cada pocos años

La clave es elegir el camino correcto alineado al modelo de negocio.

 

#CFO #SAP #S4HANA #FinanzasEstratégicas #TransformacionFinanciera #ERP #AI

entry.title: One Delivery Order with multiple PO numbers generates two invoices
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
We’re encountering a scenario where one Delivery Order (DO) contains multiple PO numbers, but during billing, SAP generates two separate invoices with different billing document numbers, and both get printed.
Business expectation is to have a single consolidated invoice (single billing document / single print copy) for the same DO.
Ideally:

1 Delivery Order → 1 Invoice

Could you please advise:
1. What standard billing split criteria could cause this behavior when multiple PO numbers exist?
2. Which configurations should we review (e.g. VTFL, billing split analysis, header/item split criteria)?
3. Is this achievable via standard configuration, or would enhancement/user exit be required?

This is not SmartForms related. It appears to be standard SD billing logic.
Appreciate any guidance or similar experience
Thank you!

entry.title: Supply Protection Extension Rule in SAP S/4 HANA
entry.content:
In this blog we will see how supply protection extension rules can be configured to modify the consumption and restriction behavior of protection groups and their corresponding time buckets.

Pre-Requisites:

  • Product version is S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition 2023 FPS03 / or above and Fashion business function is activated
  • Self-Restriction type is set as ‘Restriction Outside Planned Protection’

Solution Context:

Supply Protection Extension Rule aims in addressing below business requirements:

  1. Consider protected quantities of backward and forward time buckets while calculating remaining protection for the matching time buckets
  2. Consume protection from backward and forward time buckets
  3. Ignore restrictions applied due to horizontal protection up to remaining protection and consider them only for the required quantity exceeding remaining protection.

Below Example illustrates a scenario where Supply Protection Extension Rules are crucial in defining the consumption from multiple time buckets.

 

 

Feb

Mar

Apr

May

 

Supply

 

 

On Hand Stock

250

 

 

 

 

Inbound Delivery

 

225

 

 

 

Purchase Order

 

 

225

 

 

Purchase Requisition

 

 

 

225

 

 

 

Supply Protection

 

Priority

Protection Group

 

 

 

 

1

ECOM

50

50

50

50

1

RETAIL

75

75

75

75

2

Key Wholesale

100

100

100

100

 

General Availability

25

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Its important to set the right “Self-Restriction” Type in above case to ensure a specific channel doesn’t overconsume the supply. For e.g. if we set “Self-Restriction” as “No Self-Restriction” then ECOM and RETAIL channels can consume all of key wholesale protection which is not always intended.

We will review this example with “Self-Restriction” set as “Outside Planned Protection” which aims to ensure even low priority protection groups get their share of supply.

 Step 1: Create Supply Protection Extension Rule

  • Go to Fiori App “Configure Supply Protection Extension Rules”
  • Click on “Create” and enter below configuration required in the rule

  • Lets us understand the specifics of each field
    • General Information:
      • Rule Name – Provide a name for the extension rule to be used in “Manage Supply Protection” Fiori App.
    • Restriction Horizon:
      • Format – Here you can specify if you can borrow units from forward and backward time buckets in “Days” or “Time buckets”
      • Backward Period – How many backward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
      • Forward Period – How many forward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
    • Consumption:
      • Consumption Reference Type: Here you can specify if you want to consume based on “standard” (i.e. all units consumed only from matching time bucket) or “Restriction Horizon” (apply backward/forward horizons for consumption)
      • Consumption Direction: currently 2 types of consumption directions are support –
        • Backward then Forward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket – 1 -> Matching time bucket – 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets). Any left-over unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> Feb (50) -> April (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
        • Forward then Backward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket - 1 -> Matching time bucket - 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets). Any leftover unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> April (50) -> Feb (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
      • Priority Restriction:
        • Special Handling of Equal Priority – This indicator specifies if restrictions of “equal priority” should be considered only in the “out plan” quantity or both “in plan and out plan” quantities. i.e. If an ECOM order comes for 200 units, then “in plan” quantity is “150” and out plan is “50”:
          • If flag is not set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable for both “in plan” and “out plan” quantities
          • If flag is set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable only for “out plan” quantities

Step 2: Attach Supply Protection Extension Rule to Supply Protection Object

  • Go to Fiori App “Manage Supply Protection” and define below parameters as shown below: (Note – Attach the extension rule)
  • Note - Set Self Restriction Type as "Restriction Outside Planned Protection"

 

 

  • Activate the supply protection object

Case 1: Let’s take an example where ECOM Sales Order comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “150” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 ECOM order request is checked in 2 steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

150

No restrictions applied

150

Out Plan Quantity

50

-          Equal Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Low Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

-          Self-Restriction – 50 from ECOM April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

175 units

Case 2: Let’s take an example where RETAIL STO comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “225” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 RETAIL order request is checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

200

No restrictions applied

200

Final Confirmations

 

 

200 units

Case 3: Let’s take an example where Key Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “300” (including Feb and April). So, the 500 Key Wholesale order request checked in two steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

300

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

300

Out Plan Quantity

200

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Self-Restriction – 100 from Key Wholesale April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

325 units

Case 4: Let’s take an example where regular Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since there is no protection for regular wholesale order, all 500 units will be checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

Request Quantity

500

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          High Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

25 units

Hope this blog helps to provide a clear understanding of “Supply Protection Extension Rules” to define appropriate settings to prioritize different channels as per business requirements.

SAP Help Documentation:

  1. Supply Protection
  2. Supply Protection Extension Rule

SAP Community Blogs:

  1. Supply Protection In a Nutshell
  2. Increasing Reliability with Supply Protection
  3. Restriction by Own Protection in Supply Protection
  4. Insights on Supply Protection using Temporary Assignments

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered Chemical Quality Management System: Part 1 - SAP CAP Backend Setup
entry.content:
Introduction

In this three-part series, we'll build an intelligent chemical quality management system that combines SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Model with AI agents powered by LangChain and SAP AI Core. This system will automatically detect quality issues, recommend mitigation plans, and execute corrective actions.

What You'll Build:
